_meta.authoringNotes: Citations cross-verified against AAD/ACDS 2014 contact dermatitis guideline (Fonacier J Allergy Clin Immunol Pract 2015, PMID 25965350), ESCD European S1 hand eczema guideline (Thyssen Contact Dermatitis 2022, PMID 34971008), Apfelbacher chronic hand eczema prevalence (BJD 2025, PMID 39797908), Slodownik occupational ICD review (Contact Dermatitis 2008, PMID 18205807), Diepgen J Eur Acad Dermatol Venereol 2007 occupational hand eczema (PMID 17655621), Tan Curr Allergy Asthma Rep 2014 ICD pathophysiology (PMID 24470264), NIOSH dermal exposure database, Bauer Lancet 2010 hand eczema review (PMID 21036218). Drug names and doses cross-checked against UpToDate Lexicomp.

h1Title: Irritant Contact Dermatitis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Irritant contact dermatitis (ICD) is non-immune-mediated skin inflammation caused by direct chemical, physical, or mechanical injury to the epidermal barrier. Unlike its allergic counterpart, it can occur on first exposure in anyone given a high enough dose, requires no prior sensitization, and produces burning and stinging that dominates over itch. ICD is the most common form of contact dermatitis and accounts for roughly 80% of all occupational skin disease, with hairdressers, nurses, cleaners, food handlers, and metal workers carrying chronic hand eczema rates of 10-30%. Water, soaps, detergents, solvents, alcohols, acids, alkalis, fiberglass, friction, and low humidity are the dominant triggers. Patch testing is negative because the disease is innate, not T-cell-mediated, and management hinges on barrier repair, allergen and irritant avoidance, and topical anti-inflammatory therapy applied for 2-6 weeks until the stratum corneum recovers.

keyStats.prevalence: Chronic hand eczema affects 5-9% of European adults at any time (Apfelbacher BJD 2025); ICD accounts for roughly 80% of occupational skin disease
keyStats.demographics: Women affected 1.5-2 fold more than men, largely through wet-work occupations and household exposure; occupational cases cluster in workers under age 35
keyStats.avgAge: First episode usually within 2 years of starting a wet-work or chemical-exposure occupation; chronic disease persists into later working life
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 35 million adults globally with chronic hand eczema; ICD is the single largest contributor (Diepgen JEADV 2007)

definition: Irritant contact dermatitis (ICD-10: L24.9) is a non-immune-mediated eczematous reaction caused by the direct toxic effect of chemicals, physical agents, or mechanical forces on the keratinocytes and the lipid lamellae of the stratum corneum. Unlike allergic contact dermatitis, no antigen presentation or memory T-cell response is required — the disease is innate, occurs on first exposure if the dose is high enough, and is strictly dose-dependent. Acute high-concentration exposures (strong acids, alkalis, organic solvents) produce a sharply demarcated chemical-burn-like reaction within minutes to hours. Cumulative low-grade exposures (water, soaps, detergents, friction, low humidity) produce a slow-evolving xerotic, fissured, lichenified picture over weeks to months as repeated micro-injuries outpace barrier repair. The underlying mechanism is keratinocyte release of preformed cytokines (IL-1alpha, IL-18, TNF-alpha) and activation of the NLRP3 inflammasome when damaged cells leak ATP and uric acid as damage-associated molecular patterns. The condition is staged into acute, subacute, and cumulative chronic forms and is managed primarily by dermatology and occupational medicine, with primary care heavily involved. It sits inside the broader contact dermatitis family alongside allergic contact dermatitis (Type IV hypersensitivity), contact urticaria (immediate IgE-mediated), and photo-contact reactions. The shared end pathway is barrier breakdown plus innate immune activation, but the absence of prior sensitization and the negative patch test distinguish ICD from its allergic counterpart and shape an entirely different management plan focused on barrier restoration rather than allergen identification.

typesClassification.description: Sharply demarcated erythema, edema, bullae, and necrosis within minutes to hours of a high-dose chemical exposure such as a strong acid, alkali, or organic solvent splash. Resembles a chemical burn. Resolves over 1-2 weeks with barrier care once exposure ceases.

Erythema and vesiculation appearing 8-24 hours after a single sub-burn exposure to compounds such as benzalkonium chloride, anthralin, or hydrofluoric acid. Easily mistaken for allergic contact dermatitis because of the delay; patch testing is negative.

The dominant form — slowly evolving xerotic, scaly, fissured plaques on hands, forearms, and face from weeks to months of repeated low-grade exposure to water, soaps, detergents, friction, and dry air. Occupational hand eczema is the textbook example.

Burning, stinging, or tightness without visible inflammation, reported within minutes of applying a product. Common with cosmetics containing lactic acid, glycolic acid, retinoids, propylene glycol, or alcohol. Skin examination is normal.

Combines mechanical trauma (abrasion, burn, surgical wound) with subsequent low-grade chemical exposure. Heals slowly and is prone to lichenification; common in healthcare workers wearing tape and gloves over recent injuries.
Sterile pustules on hair-bearing or seborrheic skin from heavy oils, tars, halogens, or metals. Seen in mechanics, road workers, and shipyard workers.

primarySymptoms: Burning, stinging, or rawness over the contact area, dominant over itch, often beginning within minutes of exposure in acute cases and within hours-to-days in cumulative cases — the cardinal feature distinguishing ICD from allergic contact dermatitis.
Dryness, scaling, and rough sandpaper-like texture across the dorsal hands, finger webs, and wrists in chronic occupational cases, with skin that visibly cracks when stretched.
Erythematous patches with less sharply demarcated borders than allergic disease, often blending into surrounding normal skin rather than mapping precisely to a contact pattern.
Painful fissures over knuckles, finger pulps, and finger webs that bleed with movement and limit hand function, especially during winter months and in low-humidity environments.
Vesicles, bullae, or frank skin necrosis in acute high-concentration chemical exposures, resembling a thermal or chemical burn at the splash site.
Sterile pustules and acneiform papules in workers exposed to heavy oils, coolants, tars, halogens, or insoluble metal compounds.
Glazed or parchment-like atrophic appearance over the finger pulps in long-standing cumulative cases ("pulpitis"), with loss of skin markings and persistent dryness.
Nail-fold inflammation (paronychia-like), ridging of the nail plate, and koilonychia in chronic wet-work occupational disease.
Hyperpigmentation or hypopigmentation persisting after inflammation resolves, especially in skin of color, often visible months after barrier recovery.
Improvement on days off work, weekends, and holidays followed by recurrence within 1-2 days of returning to the irritant exposure — a near-pathognomonic occupational pattern.
earlyWarningSigns: Tightness, dryness, or rough texture on the dorsal hands within the first 1-2 months of starting a wet-work job such as nursing, hairdressing, food preparation, or cleaning
Recurrent fissuring of the finger pulps or knuckles during winter that resolves with emollient use and recurs each cold season
Stinging or burning when applying a previously well-tolerated cosmetic, sunscreen, or alcohol-based hand sanitiser
Subtle erythema and scaling under a wedding ring, watchband, or repeated medical tape application that improves over weekends
Workers reporting that several colleagues in the same workplace develop hand redness within months of starting — pattern suggests a shared irritant rather than allergic sensitization
emergencySigns: Deep burn-like lesions, full-thickness skin loss, or systemic symptoms after a strong acid, alkali, hydrofluoric acid, or organic solvent splash — irrigate immediately and seek emergency burn unit care
Hydrofluoric acid exposure of any concentration with progressive pain, fluorosis risk, or hypocalcaemia — calcium gluconate gel and emergency review even when initial skin appears mild
Spreading honey-coloured crusting, fever, and lymphangitic streaking suggesting Staphylococcus aureus superinfection or cellulitis needing oral or intravenous antibiotics
Sudden painful clustered punched-out vesicles superimposed on dermatitic skin — possible eczema herpeticum, a dermatologic emergency requiring intravenous acyclovir
Eye irritation, throat tightness, or breathing difficulty after inhaling the same chemical that caused skin dermatitis — emergency review for combined dermal and respiratory chemical injury

causes.description: Prolonged or repeated water contact (over 2 hours per day or more than 20 hand-washing episodes per shift) strips intercellular lipids and disrupts stratum corneum cohesion. Wet work is the single largest contributor to occupational hand ICD in healthcare, food, and cleaning workers (Diepgen JEADV 2007).

Anionic surfactants such as sodium lauryl sulfate solubilize stratum corneum lipids and denature keratin filaments. Each additional 10 hand washes per shift roughly doubles cumulative hand-eczema risk; alcohol-based hand sanitisers are paradoxically gentler on the barrier than soap-and-water washes when used correctly.

Aromatic and aliphatic hydrocarbons (toluene, xylene, petroleum distillates), alcohols (isopropanol, ethanol), and chlorinated solvents extract skin lipids on contact. Hairdressers, mechanics, painters, and laboratory workers are at highest risk; even brief unprotected exposure produces measurable transepidermal water loss for 24-48 hours.

Acids (hydrofluoric, hydrochloric, sulfuric, chromic) denature surface proteins and produce coagulative necrosis. Alkalis (sodium hydroxide, lime, cement, oven cleaners) cause deeper liquefactive necrosis because they saponify lipids and penetrate further. Cement burns are an under-recognised cause of severe ICD on the lower legs of construction workers kneeling in wet cement.

causes.cause: Fiberglass, mineral wool, and other physical irritants
causes.description: Small fibres mechanically abrade and embed in the stratum corneum, producing intensely itchy follicular papules and erythema on the neck, forearms, and waist. Insulation installers, glass-handling workers, and clothing assembly workers are the classic groups affected.

Repetitive mechanical force without chemical injury alone produces a hyperkeratotic, lichenified, fissured plaque — callus formation taken to a pathological extreme. Common on the palms of musicians and labourers, the fingertips of fingerprint-pad workers, and the heels of athletes.

Indoor relative humidity below 30% (winter heating, long-haul flights, semiconductor clean rooms) lowers stratum corneum water content and amplifies the irritancy of every other exposure. Many "chronic hand eczema" patients flare seasonally on this basis alone.

Childhood lip-licking dermatitis, infant napkin (diaper) dermatitis, and incontinence-associated dermatitis are textbook ICD presentations. Digestive enzymes, urea, and ammonia in body fluids irritate already-occluded skin, especially when occluded by nappies, pads, or surgical drapes.

Capsaicin (chili peppers), garlic and onion juice, daffodil and tulip sap, sea-urchin spines, and certain caterpillar hairs produce non-allergic toxic dermatitis on exposed skin. Farmers, florists, and food handlers see this regularly.

riskFactors.factor: Occupations with chronic wet work or chemical exposure
riskFactors.description: Healthcare workers, hairdressers, cleaners, food handlers, metal workers, construction workers, and mechanics carry chronic hand eczema rates of 10-30%; over 80% of occupational ICD in most national registries falls on these groups (Slodownik Contact Dermatitis 2008).

riskFactors.factor: Pre-existing atopic dermatitis or filaggrin loss-of-function
riskFactors.description: Atopic dermatitis and filaggrin null mutations are the strongest individual risk factors. Atopic workers entering wet-work occupations carry roughly 2-3 fold higher cumulative ICD risk than non-atopic peers across multiple prospective cohort studies.

Women carry roughly 1.5-2 fold higher prevalence of hand ICD than men, driven by occupational distribution (healthcare, hairdressing, cleaning, childcare) and additional household wet-work exposure. The biology of the skin barrier itself is similar between sexes.

Most occupational ICD presents within the first 2 years of starting a wet-work job. Younger skin tolerates the early insult less well, and apprenticeship years carry the highest exposure intensity before workplace habits are established.

More than 20 hand-washing episodes per shift, particularly with soap-and-water rather than alcohol-based sanitiser, roughly triples cumulative ICD risk. Pandemic-era data from 2020-2022 showed measurable rises in healthcare-worker hand dermatitis tied to hygiene intensification.

riskFactors.factor: Cold dry climates and indoor heating
riskFactors.description: Indoor humidity below 30% during winter raises transepidermal water loss and amplifies every other exposure. Workers in semiconductor clean rooms, aircrew, and northern-latitude residents flare predictably each winter.

riskFactors.factor: Concomitant exposure to multiple low-grade irritants
riskFactors.description: Workers who combine wet work, gloves, detergents, and cold air — a typical hospital cleaner or kitchen porter — carry far higher risk than the sum of single exposures; the interactions are super-additive.

Workplaces without accelerator-free nitrile gloves, cotton glove liners, pre-shift barrier creams, and structured emollient routines have several-fold higher hand ICD rates than workplaces that adopt full protective programmes.

riskFactors.factor: History of childhood eczema or sensitive skin
riskFactors.description: Self-reported sensitive skin and a childhood history of eczema carry roughly 2 fold higher risk of developing occupational ICD even when atopic dermatitis is no longer active.
Smoking modestly impairs barrier recovery and is associated with a 1.3-1.5 fold higher prevalence of chronic hand eczema in occupational cohorts.

affectedDemographics: Chronic hand eczema affects 5-9% of European adults at any time (Apfelbacher BJD 2025); ICD is the single largest contributor to that burden
Roughly 80% of all occupational skin disease in the United States and Europe is ICD; allergic contact dermatitis makes up most of the remainder
Women carry 1.5-2 fold higher prevalence than men, driven by occupational distribution (healthcare, hairdressing, cleaning, childcare) and household wet work
Healthcare workers, hairdressers, food handlers, cleaners, and metal workers carry hand-eczema rates of 10-30% in occupational surveillance data
Infant napkin (diaper) dermatitis affects up to 35% of infants at some point during the diaper-wearing years and is the leading paediatric ICD presentation
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of irritant contact dermatitis is history-driven. The clinician maps the rash distribution to the patient's exposures, notes the temporal pattern (improvement on weekends and holidays, flare on return to work), and quantifies the cumulative burden of water, soap, sanitiser, solvent, glove, friction, and low-humidity contact. A burning-and-stinging quality with less sharply demarcated borders, in a patient with high-burden wet-work or chemical exposure and no obvious single contact pattern, supports ICD over its allergic counterpart. There is no positive confirmatory test for ICD because the disease is innate rather than antigen-specific. Patch testing is performed in all chronic or unclear cases not to confirm ICD but to exclude allergic contact dermatitis. A negative comprehensive patch test (Core or European Baseline Series plus relevant supplemental series) in a typical history supports ICD by exclusion. Repeat open application testing of the patient's own products can document irritant potential when patch testing is negative but a single suspect product is implicated. Skin biopsy is rarely needed; histology of ICD shows spongiosis with neutrophilic infiltrate in acute lesions and lichenification in chronic ones, but cannot reliably distinguish ICD from allergic contact dermatitis or atopic dermatitis. Useful adjuncts include KOH microscopy and Wood's lamp to exclude tinea, bacterial swab to confirm impetiginization, and the dimethyl glyoxime spot test for nickel release from a suspect metal object — a positive spot test points to nickel exposure (potentially driving overlapping ACD) and reframes the differential. The decisive differentials to keep in mind are allergic contact dermatitis (positive patch test, sharply mapped borders, itch-dominant), atopic dermatitis (flexural, lifelong, atopic stigmata), psoriasis (well-demarcated plaques with silvery scale), tinea manuum (KOH-positive scale, central clearing, often unilateral), and dyshidrotic eczema (deep tapioca-pudding vesicles on palms and lateral fingers). The strongest single piece of evidence is the workplace pattern — ICD that resolves with two weeks off work and flares within days of return is essentially diagnostic.

diagnosticTests.test: Detailed exposure history and work-environment review
diagnosticTests.purpose: The single most important step. Quantifies wet-work hours per shift, glove use, soap and sanitiser frequency, solvent and chemical exposure, friction, humidity, and the temporal pattern of flares versus weekends and holidays. Directs supplemental patch-test panels and identifies modifiable workplace factors.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 30-60 minute structured interview covering occupation, daily tasks, household activities, hobbies, cosmetics, and medications. Patients are asked to bring product ingredient lists and a 2-week flare diary. Spouses and supervisors may be interviewed when work tasks are unclear.

diagnosticTests.test: Patch testing (Core or European Baseline Series, plus occupational supplements)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Performed to exclude allergic contact dermatitis rather than to confirm ICD. A negative comprehensive series in a typical history supports ICD by exclusion. Roughly 30-50% of suspected occupational ICD turns out to harbour a clinically relevant contact allergy as well.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Allergen-loaded chambers are taped to the upper back for 48 hours, then read at days 2, 4, and ideally 7. Patients keep the area dry, avoid sweating, exercise, and sun exposure. The procedure is painless; mild itch over true positives is normal.

diagnosticTests.test: Repeat open application test (ROAT) or use test
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms whether a specific product is causing irritation when history is suggestive but patch testing is negative. Useful for cosmetics, sanitisers, gloves, and topical medications.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Patients self-apply a thin film of the suspect product to a 2 cm × 2 cm area of the antecubital fossa twice a day for 7-21 days and monitor for itch, redness, or papules. A positive ROAT supports irritation; a negative test argues against the product being the cause.

diagnosticTests.test: Dimethyl glyoxime (DMG) spot test for nickel release
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies whether a suspect metal object (belt buckle, watchband, scissor handle, piercing, surgical instrument) releases enough nickel to drive contact dermatitis. A positive DMG spot reframes the differential toward overlapping nickel allergy in addition to ICD.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician swabs the metal surface with a DMG-containing solution; a pink colour develops within 30 seconds if the object releases more than 0.5 micrograms of nickel per square centimetre per week. Painless, in-clinic, takes 2 minutes.

diagnosticTests.test: KOH microscopy and Wood's lamp examination of skin scrapings
diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes tinea manuum, tinea corporis, and erythrasma when morphology is ambiguous — each can mimic chronic hand or foot ICD. Tinea manuum is classically unilateral and shows central clearing with an active scaly border.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Painless. Skin is gently scraped with a blade, mounted with 10-20% potassium hydroxide, and examined under a microscope for hyphae. The Wood's lamp is held over the lesion for 30-60 seconds in a darkened room.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies Staphylococcus aureus impetiginization in oozing, crusted, or treatment-refractory lesions and guides antibiotic choice. Methicillin-resistant strains are detected on routine culture.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A sterile swab is rolled over the lesion for a few seconds; culture results return in 24-72 hours. No special preparation needed.

diagnosticTests.test: Skin biopsy (4 mm punch) in atypical cases
diagnosticTests.purpose: Reserved for atypical, treatment-refractory, or asymmetric cases to exclude cutaneous T-cell lymphoma, psoriasis, lichen planus, or pemphigoid. Histology of ICD shows spongiosis with neutrophil-rich infiltrate in acute lesions but cannot reliably distinguish ICD from ACD.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 4 mm punch biopsy under local anaesthetic with 1-2 sutures, taking 10-15 minutes in clinic. Mild post-biopsy soreness and a small scar are expected; results return in 1-2 weeks.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of irritant contact dermatitis follows three pillars: remove or reduce the irritant exposure, repair the skin barrier, and quiet the inflammation. Exposure reduction is the only path to lasting control — patients leave the clinic with a written irritant-avoidance plan covering glove choice, hand-washing frequency, sanitiser type, work tasks, and household chemicals. Workplaces benefit from a structured TRIPCD-style programme: training, personal protective equipment with accelerator-free nitrile gloves and cotton liners, pre-shift barrier creams, post-shift emollient routines, and task rotation that limits any single worker's wet-work hours per shift. Barrier repair is the central pharmacotherapy in chronic ICD. Patients apply a thick fragrance-free emollient containing ceramides, urea 5-10%, or lactate at least 2-4 times daily, particularly after every hand-washing episode and at every shift break. Acute flares are managed with topical corticosteroids of potency matched to site — hydrocortisone 1-2.5% on face, eyelids, and folds; triamcinolone acetonide 0.1% on body sites; clobetasol propionate 0.05% short courses for thick lichenified plaques or palmoplantar disease — applied once or twice daily for 2-4 weeks, then tapered. Topical calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus 0.1% ointment, pimecrolimus 1% cream) are steroid-sparing alternatives ideal for face, eyelids, neck, folds, and chronic maintenance, with strong evidence in face and eyelid contact dermatitis. Newer non-steroid topicals — ruxolitinib 1.5% cream and crisaborole 2% ointment — are increasingly used off-label in chronic ICD. Cool wet compresses with normal saline or aluminum acetate (Burow's solution) help oozing acute lesions. Severe widespread ICD or acute chemical-burn-like disease may require a short course of oral corticosteroids (prednisone 0.5-1 mg/kg/day tapered over 14-21 days) plus emergency burn-unit care for deep injury. For chronic refractory occupational hand dermatitis where exposure reduction is incomplete, narrowband UVB or hand-and-foot UVA1 phototherapy, oral retinoid alitretinoin 30 mg daily (where licensed), and oral immunosuppressants (cyclosporine 3-5 mg/kg/day, methotrexate, azathioprine) are reasonable next steps. Dupilumab is increasingly used off-label when there is overlap with atopic disease. Antihistamines have a limited role beyond night-time sedation; antibiotics are reserved for documented bacterial superinfection. Patient education plus a written barrier-repair plan reduces flare frequency by 40-50% in randomized hand-eczema trials and is the single highest-yield long-term intervention.

medicalTreatments.name: Structured barrier-repair regimen with fragrance-free emollients (ceramides, urea 5-10%, lactate)
medicalTreatments.description: The cornerstone of ICD treatment. Patients apply a thick fragrance-free emollient at least 2-4 times daily, particularly after each hand-washing episode and at every shift break. Ointments outperform creams on fissured palmar skin; urea 5-10% formulations add humectant action without irritation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Roughly 60-70% reduction in flare frequency and improvement in hand-eczema severity scores at 12-24 weeks when combined with irritant avoidance, across randomized occupational hand-eczema trials.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical corticosteroids (hydrocortisone 1-2.5% face/folds; triamcinolone 0.1% body; clobetasol 0.05% short courses for lichenified plaques)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line anti-inflammatory therapy. Potency matched to site and severity. Applied once or twice daily during flares for 2-4 weeks, then tapered. Long-term continuous high-potency use is avoided due to atrophy, telangiectasia, and steroid-induced rosacea on the face.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clearance or near-clearance in 60-75% of mild-moderate flares within 2-4 weeks when paired with irritant avoidance and emollient use.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus 0.1% ointment or 0.03% in children; pimecrolimus 1% cream)
medicalTreatments.description: Steroid-sparing immunomodulators ideal for face, eyelids, neck, folds, and chronic maintenance where corticosteroid atrophy is a risk. Applied twice daily during flares and tapered to maintenance frequency. Mild transient burning is the main side effect.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Investigator Global Assessment clear or almost-clear in roughly 50-60% at 6 weeks in randomized trials of facial and eyelid contact dermatitis; useful in chronic hand ICD as a steroid-sparing maintenance option.

medicalTreatments.name: Cool wet compresses with normal saline or aluminum acetate (Burow's solution)
medicalTreatments.description: Adjunctive measure for acute oozing or vesicular dermatitis. Soaked cotton or gauze is applied to the affected skin for 15-20 minutes three to four times daily. Reduces stinging, dries vesicles, and improves comfort during the first 3-7 days of a severe flare.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptomatic relief in 80-90% of acute flares as an adjunct to topical or systemic anti-inflammatory therapy.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral corticosteroids (prednisone 0.5-1 mg/kg/day for 5-7 days, tapered over 14-21 days)
medicalTreatments.description: Reserved for severe widespread acute ICD, chemical-burn-like reactions, or treatment-refractory hand disease causing disability. Shorter courses risk rebound; taper over 2-3 weeks. Avoid in uncontrolled diabetes, severe osteoporosis, or active infection.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Significant clinical response in 80-90% within 3-5 days when paired with irritant removal and barrier repair.

medicalTreatments.name: Cyclosporine (3-5 mg/kg/day) for refractory chronic hand ICD
medicalTreatments.description: Rapid-acting calcineurin inhibitor used as a bridge in severe chronic hand or generalized ICD when topical therapy fails and exposure reduction is incomplete. Treatment limited to 3-6 months due to renal toxicity and hypertension; monthly creatinine and blood pressure monitoring required.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Roughly 50% reduction in dermatitis severity within 6-8 weeks in randomized hand-eczema trials (Thyssen Contact Dermatitis 2022 guideline).

medicalTreatments.name: Alitretinoin (oral 9-cis retinoic acid, 30 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Licensed in Europe and Canada for severe chronic hand eczema unresponsive to topical corticosteroids. Particularly useful in hyperkeratotic and fissured palmar ICD. Teratogenic — pregnancy prevention programme mandatory.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clear or almost-clear hands in roughly 48% of treated patients at 24 weeks versus 17% with placebo (BACH trial).

medicalTreatments.name: Phototherapy (narrowband UVB or local hand/foot UVA1 or PUVA)
medicalTreatments.description: Steroid-sparing option for chronic hand and foot ICD and widespread disease. Typically 2-3 sessions weekly for 8-16 weeks. Suppresses cutaneous T-cell and innate inflammatory responses and reduces flare frequency.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Roughly 50-60% of patients achieve 50% improvement at 12 weeks; durable response in many for several months after a completed course.

medicalTreatments.name: Dupilumab (300 mg subcutaneous every 2 weeks after 600 mg load) — off-label
medicalTreatments.description: Monoclonal antibody against the interleukin-4 receptor alpha chain. Approved for atopic dermatitis but increasingly used off-label for chronic ICD that fails standard therapy, particularly when there is overlap with atopic disease. No laboratory monitoring required.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Case series and small randomized data report 50-80% improvement in chronic refractory hand dermatitis including ICD; long-term outcomes still being characterized.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical anti-pruritic and barrier additives (urea 10%, lactic acid 5%, niacinamide)
medicalTreatments.description: Humectant and ceramide-precursor adjuncts that improve stratum corneum hydration in chronic xerotic ICD. Applied between corticosteroid doses and as long-term maintenance. Avoid on acutely fissured skin where stinging is intolerable.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest but consistent improvement in dryness, scaling, and transepidermal water loss across randomized split-hand trials when used 1-2 times daily for 8-12 weeks.

alternativeTreatments.name: Pre-shift barrier creams and accelerator-free nitrile gloves with cotton liners
alternativeTreatments.description: Workplace barrier programmes combining pre-shift application of barrier creams, accelerator-free nitrile gloves over cotton liners, and structured emollient breaks measurably reduce hand-eczema flare rates in randomized occupational trials (Bauer Lancet 2010; Thyssen Contact Dermatitis 2022).

alternativeTreatments.name: Patient education programmes and written eczema action plans
alternativeTreatments.description: Structured group education and individualized written avoidance plans reduce flare frequency by 40-50% across randomized trials in chronic hand dermatitis.

alternativeTreatments.name: Habit-reversal training for chronic scratching
alternativeTreatments.description: Targets the itch-scratch cycle in chronic lichenified disease. Small but reproducible improvements in symptom scores and quality of life when paired with active treatment.

whySeeSpecialist: Refer to a dermatologist with patch-testing experience whenever the dermatitis is chronic, recurrent, occupationally disabling, fails first-line topical therapy plus barrier repair after 4-6 weeks, or has an unusual distribution suggesting overlapping allergic contact dermatitis. Occupational physicians should be involved early in work-related cases to address workplace exposures, glove selection, task rotation, and disability paperwork. Patients with eyelid, facial, perianal, or genital ICD particularly benefit from early specialist input because of the diagnostic complexity and the risk of treatment-induced atrophy on these sites.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a board-certified dermatologist who routinely performs patch testing with both the North American Core Series and supplemental occupational series (hairdressers, healthcare, plastics-and-glues, metals). Ask whether the clinic offers ROAT testing for ambiguous products and whether the dermatologist provides a written irritant-avoidance and barrier-repair plan. For occupational cases, prioritize practices with experience in occupational dermatology and links to certified occupational physicians who can advocate at the workplace.
preventionStrategies: Limit cumulative wet-work to under 2 hours per shift and aim for fewer than 20 hand-washing episodes per shift; choose alcohol-based hand sanitiser over soap-and-water where infection control allows
Apply a fragrance-free emollient containing ceramides, urea, or lactate at least 2-4 times daily and after every hand-washing episode — the single highest-yield habit for chronic prevention
Use accelerator-free nitrile gloves with cotton liners for wet work and chemical exposure at home and at work, and replace gloves before the inner liner becomes damp
Avoid hot water and harsh detergents at home and use mechanical aids (dishwashers, washing machines, automatic taps) to displace direct skin contact wherever possible
Run a humidifier in winter to keep indoor relative humidity around 40-50% if working or living in heated low-humidity environments
Apply pre-shift barrier creams in high-exposure jobs and request employer-supplied training, gloves, and emollient stations under formal workplace skin-health programmes
lifestyleModifications: Maintain a daily fragrance-free emollient routine — a healthier barrier reduces flare frequency and lowers the risk of overlapping allergic sensitization
Wash with lukewarm rather than hot water, pat dry, and apply emollient within 3 minutes (the "soak-and-seal" approach) to lock in stratum corneum hydration
Carry a travel-size emollient and use it at every shift break or hand-washing episode while at work
Wear well-fitting cotton-lined nitrile gloves for cleaning, dishwashing, gardening, and DIY tasks; never reuse damp gloves
Keep a flare diary noting products, occupational tasks, weather, and stress — patterns often emerge that identify overlooked irritants
Address comorbid atopic dermatitis with daily emollients and proactive topical anti-inflammatory maintenance — a healthier barrier lowers the rate of both ICD and new contact sensitization
Avoid alcohol-based aftershaves, astringents, and high-strength alpha-hydroxy-acid cosmetics on active ICD; reintroduce only after the barrier has recovered

recommended: Balanced diet appropriate for age — no specific food group eliminations are required for pure ICD
Adequate vitamin D in deficient patients — modestly supports barrier integrity in randomized trials
Whole grains, vegetables, and lean protein to support overall skin and immune health
Plenty of water to maintain hydration and reduce concomitant xerosis
Omega-3 rich foods (oily fish, flaxseed, walnuts) which support skin barrier lipid composition in observational and small interventional studies
avoid: Capsaicin-heavy foods handled with bare hands at home and at work — chili oil and pepper juice produce direct irritant dermatitis on already-damaged skin
Excess alcohol — disrupts sleep, lowers itch threshold, and impairs barrier repair
Untested broad elimination diets — they cause nutritional gaps without reducing ICD severity
Highly processed foods low in essential fatty acids — modest but consistent association with poorer barrier function
Inhaled or contact exposure to volatile cooking irritants without ventilation — not strictly diet, but a frequent home trigger overlooked in food preparation

exerciseGuidelines: Exercise is safe and encouraged. Sweat alone can mildly irritate hyperreactive skin, so shower lukewarm within an hour after activity and apply emollient. Use accelerator-free gloves for sports involving rubber grips or shared equipment, wash hands after handling weights and turf, and avoid alcohol-based wipes on already-dermatitic skin. Swimmers with chlorine-related ICD should rinse and moisturize immediately on exit.
dailyManagement: Apply a thick fragrance-free emollient at least 2-4 times daily — the highest-yield habit for sustaining barrier integrity
Carry a written irritant-avoidance and barrier-repair plan and review it at every dermatology visit
Use the fingertip unit dosing method for topical corticosteroids to avoid under- or over-treatment
Wear cotton-lined accelerator-free nitrile gloves during wet work and chemical exposure at home and at work; replace gloves before the inner liner becomes damp
Maintain a flare action plan: at the first sign of dryness, fissuring, or burning, restart the prescribed flare regimen and tighten irritant avoidance rather than waiting
prognosis: With effective irritant reduction and a structured barrier-repair regimen, the prognosis for irritant contact dermatitis is excellent. Most acute flares resolve within 2-4 weeks once exposure is reduced and topical anti-inflammatory therapy is started. Long-term clearance depends on how comprehensively the irritant burden can be reduced in daily life — occupational hand dermatitis is the most prognostically difficult subset, with persistent disease in 30-50% even after workplace modifications, and 5-10% of severely affected workers eventually leaving their occupation. ICD itself does not shorten life, but chronic hand or face disease carries measurable burden — significant impact on work, household tasks, and quality-of-life scores comparable to moderate psoriasis, plus increased rates of anxiety and depression. Crucially, longstanding ICD raises the probability of developing overlapping allergic contact dermatitis because a damaged barrier allows greater allergen penetration; up to 30-50% of chronic occupational ICD patients are found to harbour a clinically relevant contact allergy on patch testing. The decisive prognostic factor is not the initial severity but how well irritant reduction and barrier repair are sustained over months and years. Children with infant napkin dermatitis recover fully once nappy habits are corrected.
recoveryTimeline: An acute ICD flare typically responds within days of irritant removal and topical corticosteroid initiation: visible redness drops within 3-5 days, burning within 5-10 days, and most flares clear within 2-4 weeks. Chronic lichenified plaques take longer — 4-12 weeks of consistent therapy plus irritant reduction. Post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation can persist for 2-6 months after the active dermatitis resolves, especially in skin of color. After comprehensive workplace intervention, most occupational patients see meaningful improvement within 1-3 months and full re-equilibration of skin barrier over 3-6 months. Re-exposure at any point can reproduce the original eruption within days.
complications: Chronic lichenified, fissured hand or foot dermatitis with significant occupational disability — preventable with early diagnosis and structured barrier programmes
Secondary allergic contact dermatitis from increased allergen penetration through a damaged barrier — up to 30-50% of chronic ICD patients are patch-test positive to a relevant allergen
Bacterial superinfection (Staphylococcus aureus impetiginization or cellulitis) over fissured skin, sometimes producing systemic illness
Eczema herpeticum — disseminated herpes simplex virus infection of dermatitic skin, a dermatologic emergency requiring intravenous acyclovir
Post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation or hypopigmentation, particularly in skin of color, persisting for months after active disease resolves
Loss of occupation in 5-10% of severely affected workers with refractory hand dermatitis, with significant financial and psychological consequences
Topical-corticosteroid-induced atrophy, telangiectasia, or rosacea-like reaction from prolonged misuse, particularly on the face and eyelids
Sleep disturbance, anxiety, and depression in chronic disease, with quality-of-life scores comparable to moderate psoriasis

supportResources.name: American Contact Dermatitis Society (ACDS)
supportResources.description: Patient resources, FAQs, and the CAMP database for personalized product avoidance when overlapping allergic sensitization is identified.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Dermatology — Contact Dermatitis
supportResources.description: Authoritative US patient education from the body that publishes dermatology guidelines.

supportResources.name: DermNet — Irritant Contact Dermatitis
supportResources.description: Detailed peer-reviewed clinical and patient-facing content used by clinicians worldwide.

supportResources.name: National Eczema Association — Hand Eczema
supportResources.description: US-based patient advocacy organization with evidence-based education on hand eczema and contact dermatitis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Allergic Contact Dermatitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Allergic contact dermatitis is a delayed-type (Type IV) hypersensitivity that requires prior sensitization and produces an itch-dominant vesicular reaction 24-72 hours after re-exposure with sharply demarcated borders and positive patch testing. Irritant contact dermatitis is non-immune-mediated, occurs on first exposure if the dose is high enough, is burning-dominant rather than itch-dominant, has less sharply demarcated borders, and is patch-test negative. The two frequently coexist, especially in occupational hand disease.

confusedWithConditions.name: Atopic Dermatitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Atopic dermatitis is a lifelong, flexural, itchy condition that usually starts in infancy and is accompanied by personal or family atopy (asthma, hay fever, food allergy). ICD often starts in adulthood, maps to occupational or environmental irritant exposure rather than flexural creases, and improves on weekends and holidays. Atopic dermatitis raises ICD risk roughly 2-3 fold because of an inherently weaker barrier, and the two commonly coexist.

confusedWithConditions.name: Psoriasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Psoriasis presents as well-demarcated thick red plaques with silvery scale on extensor surfaces, scalp, and the lumbosacral area, with nail pitting and arthritis in many patients. ICD favours sites of irritant contact, lacks the typical silvery scale, often shows fissures rather than thick plaques, and improves with barrier care rather than calcipotriol or biologic therapy.

confusedWithConditions.name: Seborrheic Dermatitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Seborrheic dermatitis produces greasy yellow scale on sebum-rich areas (scalp, eyebrows, nasolabial folds, chest) driven by an inflammatory response to Malassezia yeast. ICD typically lacks the greasy scale, follows an irritant exposure pattern, and improves with barrier care and irritant reduction rather than antifungal shampoos and creams.

relatedConditions.name: Allergic Contact Dermatitis
relatedConditions.relevance: The other half of the contact dermatitis family; immune-mediated, sensitization-dependent, and frequently coexists with ICD in occupational hand disease.

relatedConditions.name: Atopic Dermatitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Filaggrin loss-of-function and active atopic dermatitis raise ICD risk 2-3 fold by weakening the stratum corneum barrier.

relatedConditions.name: Eczema
relatedConditions.relevance: Umbrella term that encompasses ICD; readers searching 'eczema' frequently have irritant or contact dermatitis identified only after a careful exposure history.

relatedConditions.name: Occupational Dermatitis
relatedConditions.relevance: ICD is the single largest contributor to occupational skin disease in healthcare, hairdressing, food preparation, cleaning, and metalwork.

relatedConditions.name: Occupational Chemical Burns
relatedConditions.relevance: Acute high-concentration ICD shades into chemical burn injury; both share the immediate-irrigation principle and may require burn-unit referral.

relatedConditions.name: Seborrheic Dermatitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Common facial differential in adults; both can coexist and respond to overlapping anti-inflammatory and barrier-repair therapy.

faqs.question: Is irritant contact dermatitis curable?
faqs.answer: Yes — irritant contact dermatitis is fully reversible once exposure is reduced and the barrier is restored. Most patients clear within 2-4 weeks of starting daily emollient and topical anti-inflammatory therapy. Unlike allergic disease, no lifelong immunologic memory develops.

faqs.question: How do I know if my rash is irritant or allergic contact dermatitis?
faqs.answer: Irritant disease is burning-dominant, dose-dependent, and patch-test negative. Allergic disease is itch-dominant, develops 24-72 hours after re-exposure in a sensitized patient, and is patch-test positive. The two often coexist; only patch testing in a dermatology clinic tells them apart reliably.

faqs.question: What are the most common causes of irritant contact dermatitis?
faqs.answer: The leading causes are water and frequent hand-washing, soaps and detergents, organic solvents, acids and alkalis, fiberglass, friction, and low humidity. Healthcare workers, hairdressers, cleaners, food handlers, and metal workers are the highest-risk occupations.

faqs.question: Can I get irritant contact dermatitis on first exposure?
faqs.answer: Yes. Unlike allergic disease, irritant contact dermatitis needs no prior sensitization. A high-enough single exposure to an acid, alkali, or solvent injures anyone's skin. Cumulative low-grade exposures overwhelm barrier repair over weeks. Dose-dependence is the defining feature.

faqs.question: Why is my hand eczema worse at work and better on weekends?
faqs.answer: Improvement on weekends and flares within 1-2 days of returning to work is a near-pathognomonic pattern of occupational irritant contact dermatitis. The barrier partially recovers when exposure stops, then breaks down again. Documenting this in a diary strongly supports the diagnosis.

faqs.question: Will patch testing help diagnose irritant contact dermatitis?
faqs.answer: Patch testing is negative in pure ICD because the disease is innate, not antigen-specific. It is still done in chronic cases to exclude allergic contact dermatitis, which often coexists. A negative comprehensive panel in a typical history supports ICD by exclusion.

faqs.question: How long does irritant contact dermatitis take to heal?
faqs.answer: Acute flares clear within 2-4 weeks once irritant exposure is reduced and topical anti-inflammatory therapy is started. Redness drops within 3-5 days, burning within 5-10 days. Chronic lichenified plaques take 4-12 weeks. Post-inflammatory pigment change can persist for months.

faqs.question: Are alcohol-based hand sanitisers worse than soap?
faqs.answer: No — alcohol-based sanitisers are paradoxically gentler on the barrier than soap-and-water when used correctly. Soap strips stratum corneum lipids more aggressively. Healthcare workers with hand dermatitis should switch to sanitiser where infection control allows and apply emollient afterwards.

faqs.question: What is the best moisturiser for irritant contact dermatitis?
faqs.answer: Use a thick fragrance-free emollient containing ceramides, urea 5-10%, glycerin, or lactate. Ointments outperform creams on fissured palms. Apply at least 2-4 times daily and after every hand-wash. Avoid fragranced lotions and strong alpha-hydroxy-acid products on active dermatitis.

faqs.question: Can children get irritant contact dermatitis?
faqs.answer: Yes — infant napkin (diaper) dermatitis affects up to 35% of infants and is the leading paediatric form. Childhood lip-licking dermatitis is another classic example. Older children develop ICD from sports tape, friction, pool chlorine, and astringent cosmetics.

faqs.question: What should I do if I cannot reduce the irritant at work?
faqs.answer: Talk to your dermatologist and occupational physician. Useful adjustments include task rotation, accelerator-free nitrile gloves with cotton liners, pre-shift barrier creams, sanitiser over soap, and emollient breaks. Refractory cases may need systemic therapy and disability assessment.

faqs.question: When is irritant contact dermatitis a medical emergency?
faqs.answer: Seek same-day care after acid, alkali, hydrofluoric acid, or solvent splashes, deep burn-like lesions, spreading honey-coloured crusting with fever, sudden painful clustered vesicles (possible eczema herpeticum), or airway irritation. Hydrofluoric acid needs ER review even if skin looks mild.

faqs.question: Does irritant contact dermatitis increase my risk of allergies?
faqs.answer: Yes. A damaged barrier allows greater allergen penetration, so longstanding ICD raises the chance of overlapping allergic contact dermatitis. Up to 30-50% of chronic occupational ICD patients are patch-test positive — which is why patch testing is recommended in chronic disease.

faqs.question: Can stress make irritant contact dermatitis worse?
faqs.answer: Stress does not cause ICD but amplifies flares. Stress releases neuropeptides that lower the itch threshold and disrupt the barrier, and sleep loss intensifies scratching. Habit-reversal training, CBT, and sleep hygiene reduce flare frequency in chronic disease.

faqs.question: Are steroid creams safe for irritant contact dermatitis?
faqs.answer: Yes, when used correctly. Match potency to site (low on face and folds, mid on body, high for thick plaques), apply once or twice daily for 2-4 weeks, then taper. Long-term high-potency use on the face causes atrophy, so calcineurin inhibitors are preferred for facial maintenance.

faqs.question: How much does treatment for irritant contact dermatitis cost?
faqs.answer: Most treatment is inexpensive. Topical corticosteroids, emollients, and barrier creams are widely available as low-cost generics. Dermatology visits and patch testing range from USD 200-1500 in the US and are typically free under European public systems. Phototherapy and systemics cost more.

faqs.question: Can pregnant women treat irritant contact dermatitis safely?
faqs.answer: Yes. Fragrance-free emollients, low-to-mid potency topical corticosteroids on small areas, and narrowband UVB are considered safe in pregnancy. Calcineurin inhibitors are avoided; oral retinoids and JAK inhibitors are contraindicated. Severe flares may need a short systemic steroid course.

faqs.question: Is fiberglass a common cause of irritant contact dermatitis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Fiberglass and mineral wool produce itchy follicular papules on the neck, forearms, and waist of insulation installers and glass workers. Fibres mechanically abrade the stratum corneum. Wash with cool water and change clothes; avoid hot showers, which open follicles and worsen irritation.

faqs.question: Why does winter make my hand dermatitis worse?
faqs.answer: Indoor humidity below 30% during winter heating lowers stratum corneum water content and amplifies the irritancy of soap, water, and friction. Cold air and hot showers compound the effect. A humidifier set to 40-50% and increased emollient use measurably reduce winter flares.

faqs.question: Are there any home remedies that help irritant contact dermatitis?
faqs.answer: Evidence-supported measures include fragrance-free emollient twice daily, the soak-and-seal technique (lukewarm wash then emollient within 3 minutes), cool oat or saline compresses for flares, and humidified indoor air. Avoid essential oils, vinegar, and lemon juice — they frequently worsen ICD.

faqs.question: Can irritant contact dermatitis lead to job loss?
faqs.answer: Yes — roughly 5-10% of severely affected workers with refractory hand dermatitis eventually leave their occupation. The financial and psychological burden is substantial. Early diagnosis, workplace barrier programmes, accelerator-free nitrile gloves, and specialist co-management cut this risk.

faqs.question: Why does my baby get a rash in the nappy area?
faqs.answer: Infant napkin (diaper) dermatitis is a textbook irritant contact dermatitis from prolonged urine and faeces contact under an occlusive nappy, with friction adding injury. Frequent changes, gentle cleansing, zinc oxide or petrolatum, and short nappy-free intervals usually clear it within a week.

images.altText: Clinical photograph of irritant contact dermatitis on the dorsal hands showing erythema, dryness, and fissures typical of occupational wet-work exposure
images.caption: Irritant contact dermatitis on the dorsal hands — the classic occupational presentation with dryness, erythema, and finger-web fissures.

images.altText: Chronic hand eczema with fissuring and lichenification on the palmar surface, typical of cumulative irritant contact dermatitis in a wet-work occupation
images.caption: Chronic hand eczema with palmar fissures and lichenification — the cumulative form of irritant contact dermatitis.

images.altText: Infant napkin (diaper) dermatitis with sharply demarcated erythema sparing skin folds, the textbook paediatric presentation of irritant contact dermatitis
images.caption: Infant napkin (diaper) dermatitis — a textbook irritant contact dermatitis from urine, faeces, and friction under occlusion.

images.altText: Patch test chambers applied to the upper back to distinguish allergic from irritant contact dermatitis
images.caption: Patch testing on the upper back — used in irritant contact dermatitis to exclude an overlapping allergic component.
